title: PA State Rep.JohnLawrenceServing Pennsylvania's 13th Legislative District
raw_text:
Jun
10
Press Release
Friel, Lawrence: Bill modernizing lending for Pa. farmers moves from committee
Read More

ai_summary.summary: A bill related to modernizing lending practices for Pennsylvania farmers advanced out of committee. Legislators Friel and Lawrence are associated with moving this legislation forward through the committee process.

ai_summary.summary: This is not a press release but rather a website widget for a Pennsylvania House member's email newsletter signup page. No legislative action, statement, or event is described. There is no substantive content to summarize.

title: In the News
raw_text:
Lawrence in the News
It is an honor and a privilege to work for you and all residents of the 13th Legislative District in the Pennsylvania House of Representatives. One way that I keep you informed about my work on your behalf is to post news coverage of events and initiatives in which I am involved. As always, feel free to contact me for more information about these or any state-related matter.
House Committee Passes Bill Permitting Schools to Dispense Whole Milk
Philip Gruber
Lancaster Farming News
April 30, 2024
Ready the complete story here
Capitolwire: Push to allow whole milk with school lunches is giving district officials heartburn.
By
John Finnerty
Capitolwire.com Bureau Chief

Pennsylvania House Passes Bill To Expand Coronvirus Testing
Jere Gish
, WGAL News 8
The house passed legislation Monday the would establish a framework for statewide coronavirus testing.
Read the complete story here
.
A bill to allow local health departments to perform COVID-19 antibody tests to be voted on by the House on Monday
Author:
Chelsea Koerbler (FOX43)
The legislation would allow them to do so. The tests would just require a drop of someone's blood to test if they have antibodies built up of the virus.
Read the complete story here
.

By Michael P. RellahanStaff Writer
Bipartisan legislation to address the obstacles that Chester County officials say are delaying their plan to use antibody...
Read the complete story here
.
Call and email volume up at local representatives offices
By:
BILL RETTEW
During the coronavirus pandemic, state representatives and senators say their offices are busier than ever.
Read the complete story here
.
Pa.вАЩs agriculture secretary confronted about past opposition to cutting horse racing subsidies
By Ed Mahon
Pennsylvania Agriculture Secretary Russell Redding supports a proposal to take about $200 million from the racehorse industry to fund college scholarships.

.
GOP lawmakers spar with Secretary Topper on Farm Show, HealthChoices in budget hearing
By Steve Bittenbender |
Watchdog.org
The Pennsylvania state House Appropriations Committee hearing Wednesday on the Department of General Services (DGS) started contentiously.

Chester County Highway dedicated in memory of fallen Trooper Iwaniec
By Ginger Rae Dunbar
gdunbar@21st-centurymedia.com
@GingerDunbar
on Twitter
A memorial was unveiled Monday in honor of fallen Trooper Kenton Iwaniec, who вАЬlived to serve and protect our community.вАЭ
Read the complete story here
.
The strange case of a cyber strike that (briefly) renamed a government agency
by John Baer, STAFF COLUMNIST
baerj@phillynews.com
OK, this might be nothing. Might be something. But itвАЩs, at least, a little odd. Details of a hacked Pennsylvania Department of Health website are being closely held by the Wolf administration and raising questions at a time of worldwide jitters over cybersecurity.
Read the complete story here
.
State confirms unauthorized changes were made to vital records site
Written by
Katie Meyer, Capitol Bureau Chief
The state website where people can order birth and death records was recently offline for nearly a full week because someone made unauthorized changes to the site.
Read the complete story here
.
Lawmakers seek audit of projects that received PENNVEST funding
Published by Steven Hoffman - Chester County Press
State lawmakers are calling for Pennsylvania Auditor General Eugene DePasquale to conduct a financial audit of the 118 nonpoint source projects approved by the Pennsylvania Infrastructure Investment Authority (PENNVEST) board of directors.
Read the complete story here
.
Bills, budgets, and the never-ending balancing act of being a state lawmaker
Published by Steven Hoffman - Chester County Press
Every day is different when your workplace is the State Capitol, but this is more or less a typical day in the life of Lawrence. ItвАЩs a day that began long before he stepped into the committee meeting to discuss HR 948. Lawrence commuted to the state capital from his home in the 13th District, and there were morning meetings in his Harrisburg office?office 211 in the Ryan Office Building.
Read the complete story here
.
Toomey aide, Smucker tour downtown Oxford
By
Chris Barber
, Daily Local News
OXFORD:¬† Borough officials hosted a town tour this week with U.S. Rep. Lloyd Smucker, R-16, of Lancaster, and Kate Schramm вАФ a representative of U.S. Sen. Pat ToomeyвАЩs office вАФ with an eye toward connecting with some federal funds for OxfordвАЩs planned Multimodal Transportation Center.
Read The Complete Story Here
.

ai_summary.summary: This page is a press release aggregator from a Pennsylvania state representative in the 13th Legislative District, collecting news articles covering their involvement in various legislative activities. The most prominent recent action is a bill the legislator introduced (HB 2397) that would permit schools to serve whole milk, which passed through a House committee in April 2024. The page also references past involvement in COVID-19 testing legislation, vaccine clinic bills, and other local and state matters.

title: Issue Correspondence

.

Rep. Lawrence referenced in his questioning of Commissioner Humphreys
regarding significant auto insurance rate hikes, and follow up correspondence from the Pennsylvania Insurance Department admitting that they did, in fact, approve very significant rate hikes.

regarding the alarming increase in statewide hospital closures during 2024 Budget Hearings.

ai_summary.summary: Rep. John Lawrence conducted oversight activities across multiple issues, including questioning state agency officials during budget hearings about auto insurance rate hikes, hospital closures, and aging services, as well as filing Right-to-Know requests to obtain records about the City of Chester's financial situation and potential sale of Chester Water Authority. He also sent letters to state agencies regarding a proposed state laboratory, COVID-19 testing failures, and vaccine distribution policies. Much of his work involved using formal oversight tools like hearings, correspondence, and open records requests to obtain information that agencies initially withheld or denied having.

title: Chester Water Authority
raw_text:
Chester Water Authority
For several years, I have been leading efforts in the General Assembly to halt any sale of Chester Water Authority.¬† This website serves to provide data and information on this effort.
Back in 2020, I questioned Department of Environmental Protection (DEP) Secretary Patrick McDonnell in a House hearing after DEP took a position against CWA in a Delaware County court filing.
You can view a video of this exchange here
.
On April 22, 2020, House Speaker Bryan Cutler and I sent a letter to Department of Community and Economic Development (DCED) Secretary Dennis Davin, expressing our concern over the Wolf AdministrationвАЩs assertion that Chester Water Authority is an asset of the city of Chester.¬†You can read the letter
HERE
.
Here is DCEDвАЩs response
where they claim that they have not вАЬpromoted a sale of the {Chester Water] Authority.
While DCED was telling me they were not вАЬpromoting a saleвАЭ of CWA, they sent this letter, dated April 1, 2020, to Governor Wolf.¬† The letter makes the case for Governor Wolf to declare a fiscal emergency in the City of Chester, which he did several weeks later.
Note the third page, which includes a redacted paragraph
.¬† The sentence after the redaction, clearly referring to the redacted language, notes that Chester city issued вАЬan RFP of which they received four responses.вАЭ¬† That can only refer to one thing вАУ the Chester Water Authority.
So while telling me that they were not promoting a sale of CWA, in reality, DCED used the monetization of CWA as a basis for ChesterвАЩs fiscal emergency proclamation.
Here is a link to a 2022 budget hearing
where I asked DCED about this apparent contradiction.¬† I was stonewalled in my attempt to get answers to some very basic questions.
With regard to legislation, I drafted and introduced House Bill 2597 in 2020 to require a ratepayer referendum for the sale of any publicly managed utility, including CWA. In writing this legislation, I wanted to ensure those most directly impacted had a voice in any proposed sale of a public utility. House Bill 2597 would require a publicly managed utility contemplating selling to a for-profit corporation to mail a ballot to each ratepayer of the utility asking вАЬDo you approve the sale of (selling utility name) to (acquiring entity) for the sum of (the proposed sale price)?вАЭ Ratepayers would have a minimum of 30 days to vote via U.S. mail or through a secure internet website. In addition to mailing a ballot to each ratepayer, a selling utility would be required to publish notification of the referendum in a newspaper of general circulation in any affected municipality. The Pennsylvania Public Utility Commission (PUC) would be required to abide by the results of a customer referendum in any decision surrounding the sale of a public utility, including any proposed sale of CWA.
Here is a link to
House Bill 2597
.
I reintroduced this bill for the 2021 session as
House Bill 97
.
In this 2021 letter, DCED claims that they вАЬhave not taken any position on the sale of [Chester] Water Authority.вАЭ¬† Their
now-released April 1, 2020 letter to Governor Wolf
suggests otherwise.
House Bill 1936
, introduced by Representative Krueger and me, would prohibit the sale of a municipally owned water or wastewater system that is not in financial or operational distress.¬† If enacted, it would prevent the sale of Chester Water Authority.
Representative Krueger and I held a press conference in the state Capitol on this legislation which
can be viewed here
.
You can read more about the press conference
in this news article
.
During this yearвАЩs Budget Hearings, I asked DCED about the 1973 agreement between the City of Chester and DELCORA, which states that certain assets revert from DELCORAвАЩs ownership to the City of Chester in the event that DELCORA ceases to operate those assets.¬† DELCORA, a wasterwater treatment agency in Delaware County, is in the process of being sold to a private company.¬† While I am not necessarily in favor of a DELCORA sale, if a sale does occur, and assets revert to the City of Chester under the 1973 agreement, then the City could potentially monetize those assets and realize a significant payment вАУ perhaps as much as $100 million.¬† Incredibly, DCED maintained they had no knowledge of this, despite the fact that it could result in significant revenue to the City of Chester.¬† In my view, this is particularly egregious for several reasons.¬† First, DCED and the CityвАЩs receiver are supposed to be knowledgeable about financial possibilities for the (nearly bankrupt) city of Chester.¬† Clearly, they were not in this case.¬† Second, there has been a nearly singular focus on the sale of CWA вАУ for the claimed reason that a sale would provide badly needed cash for the City.¬† Why the singular focus on CWA, and the complete ignorance around this agreement with DELCORA?¬†A few months after I leveled these accusations at a public hearing in Harrisburg, the Receiver for the City of Chester quietly sent a letter to the PA Public Utility Commission asserting the CityвАЩs claim to assets arising from any DELCORA sale вАУ specifically noting the 1973 agreement that I highlighted.
Here is a link
to the 1973 agreement.
Here is a link
of me questioning the Secretary of DCED on this issue.
Here is a link
to a Feb. 28, 2022, letter sent from the law firm of Campbell Durrant to the Receiver for the City of Chester вАУ apparently in response to my questions during the DCED budget hearing a few weeks before.
Here is a link
to the April 2022 letter that the Receiver for the City of Chester (who is directly appointed by DCED) sent to the PUC regarding the CityвАЩs position on ownership and reversion of certain assets if a sale of DELCORA proceeds.
A number of court cases have been filed on this issue.¬† In particular, Delaware County Courts decided that the board of CWA, not the City of Chester, had the authority to approve any sale of CWA.¬† On September 16, 2021, the Commonwealth Court reversed the lower courtвАЩs ruling in a 5-2 decision and remanded the case back to county court.¬† You can read the majority and dissenting opinions issued by the Commonwealth Court at the attached link. The dissenting opinion begins on
page 32 of the 44 page pdf file
.¬† In my view, the dissenting opinion is spot-on.
The next day, Chester Water Authority filed an allocatur petition asking the Pennsylvania Supreme Court to take up a review of the case, and the decision of the Commonwealth Court.
You can read that appeal here
.
In October 2021, Representative Cutler and I filed an Application for Leave to file an Amicus Curiae Brief, also called a Friend-of-the-Court Brief, in support of Chester Water AuthorityвАЩs position to the Pennsylvania Supreme Court. You can review a copy
of our brief here
.
About a week later, the Chester County Commissioners also filed a petition asking the Pennsylvania Supreme Court to take up this issue. You can
review that document here
.
The City of Chester subsequently filed a response asking the Supreme Court to deny the application filed by Representative Cutler and me.¬† You can
review that document here
.
I believe the City of ChesterвАЩs legal reasoning is flawed.¬† With this in mind, Representative Cutler and I filed a response to Chester CityвАЩs flawed argument.¬† You can read our reply,
again filed with the Pennsylvania Supreme Court, here
.
On April 11, 2022, the Pennsylvania Supreme Court granted the petition for allowance of appeal in
In re: Chester Water Authority
.¬† I filed an Amicus Curiae brief in favor of CWA's position which
can be viewed here
.¬† The Supreme Court is expected to hear the case in late November 2022.
After the City of Chester filed for bankruptcy in Nov. 2022, I updated the House of Representatives on the latest actions and called on Gov. Tom Wolf to take any sale of CWA off the table.¬†¬†¬†You can watch that floor speech
here
.
Here is a
link
to the Amended Recovery Plan filed in Commonwealth Court by the receiver for the City of Chester in Nov. 2022.
Here is a
link
to Chester CityвАЩs Nov. 2022 motion for an order finding that 11 USC ¬І 362 does not operate as a stay of the receiverвАЩs modification of amended recovery plan pending before the Commonwealth Court of Pennsylvania.
Here is a
link
to a civil action filed by the Chester Receiver, Michael Doweary, regarding the potential sale of the DELCORA waste water system.
In April 2023, the Delco Times reported that
Receiver Doweary
broached the subject of disincorporation of the City of Chester at a Municipal Financial Recovery advisory meeting.
A few days later,
I questioned then-acting DCED Secretary Rick Siger
during a House hearing regarding his position on a potential sale of CWA, also addressing a number of related items.
At a subsequent hearing,
I asked the PUC
to state on the record that ratepayers are the ones who bear the burden of corporate purchases of municipal utilities.
In July 2023, the
Commonwealth Court issued a landmark decision
in the case Cicero V Pennsylvania Public Utility Commission, overturning the PUCвАЩs approval of the sale of East Whiteland TownshipвАЩs sewer system to corporate ownership on the basis that the sale would harm consumers and result in higher utility bills.
As of Aug. 2023, after years of review, the receiver for the City of Chester has yet to determine the revenue necessary to sustain basic services in the City of Chester.
This presentation
outlines where things stand, and
this letter
to the receiver asks for an update on this basic and important question.
In February 2024,
I questioned DCED Secretary Siger
on his department's latest thinking of any proposed sale of CWA.¬† The Secretary largely avoided my questions.
Also in February 2024, the Pennsylvania Public Utility Commission proposed revisions to the guidelines for Title 66 Section 1329 acquisitions and asked for public comment on their proposal.¬† I submitted a comment which you
can read HERE
.
Pennsylvania Supreme Court Arguments
May 14, 2025 In Re: Chester Water Auth
(Audio recording provided by the court.¬† CWA case beings at approximately 4:55:40).
Amicus Curiae Brief
filed by Rep. Lawrence and Rep. Cutler in support of CWA regarding In Re: Chester Water Auth

ai_summary.summary: A Pennsylvania state representative has spent several years opposing the potential sale of Chester Water Authority (CWA), a publicly owned water utility. Their actions include introducing legislation to require ratepayer referendums before any public utility sale, co-introducing a bill to prohibit sales of financially stable municipal water systems, filing amicus curiae briefs in Pennsylvania Supreme Court proceedings, and publicly questioning state agency officials about contradictions in their stated positions on the CWA sale. They also drew attention to a 1973 agreement that could provide the City of Chester with significant alternative revenue from a DELCORA asset sale.

Lawrence, Stender: ItвАЩs Time to Prioritize, Incentivize Dairy Processing in PA
raw_text:
Lawrence, Stender: ItвАЩs Time to Prioritize, Incentivize Dairy Processing in PA
May 15, 2025
HARRISBURG
вАУ Reps. John Lawrence (R-Chester) and Michael Stender (R-Northumberland/Montour) attempted to amend legislation in the House Tuesday to provide a long overdue and much-needed boost to PennsylvaniaвАЩs struggling dairy industry. Both measures were rejected on a 102-101 party-line vote.
The amendments were aimed at fixing the Economic Development for a Growing Economy (EDGE) Tax Credit Program, which was created in 2022 to help attract large dairy processors like the ones that are now choosing to locate in neighboring states. However, the substantial amount of investment required to qualify for the tax credit has left the program unused.
LawrenceвАЩs amendment to
House Bill 500
would make Pennsylvania more competitive by providing tax incentives to locate new or expand existing dairy processing plants to provide increased processing capacity for Pennsylvania milk. The amendment also addressed two key environmental concerns by providing tax incentives for the construction of methane digesters to reduce nutrient runoff into the Chesapeake Bay along with implementation of new technologies to reduce methane and hydrogen sulfide emissions from agricultural operations.
вАЬPennsylvania is losing out to surrounding states like New York because of our stateвАЩs failure to be competitive in tax and regulatory policies, and our family farmers are suffering as a result,вАЭ said Lawrence. вАЬMy proposal would unleash construction of new dairy processing facilities across the Commonwealth and tackle significant quality-of-life issues surrounding hydrogen sulfide emissions and other environmental priorities.¬† I am stunned that members of the state House rejected this commonsense approach on a party-line vote.вАЭ
To watch LawrenceвАЩs remarks during debate in the House,
click here
.
StenderвАЩs amendment would have likewise made key changes to the legislation to better serve the dairy industry. It proposed lowering the capital investment threshold from $50 million to $25 million and reducing the required number of new jobs in the bill from 100 to 50. These changes aimed to make the tax credit more accessible to our in-state processors, not just new large-scale operations.
вАЬThe governor said he supports farmers, but when the dairy industry asked for targeted, commonsense reforms, his administration said no,вАЭ said Stender. вАЬMy amendment would have made the tax credit work for expanding existing dairy processing instead of just massive corporate projects. House Democrats were ready to support it, but the governorвАЩs team shut it down. You canвАЩt claim to stand with agriculture while blocking the very changes Pennsylvania farmers are asking for.вАЭ
A technical amendment by Stender, requiring milk entering the processing plant to be measured by weight instead of gallons, received unanimous approval in the House. It was the only Republican amendment to House Bill 500 to be approved by the Democrat majority.
To watch StenderвАЩs remarks on his amendments,
click here
.
Representative John Lawrence
13th District
Representative Michael Stender
108th District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives
Media Contact: Donna Pinkham
717.260.6452
dpinkham@pahousegop.com
Media Contact: Rick Leiner

ai_summary.summary: Republican Representatives Lawrence and Stender proposed two amendments to House Bill 500 that would have expanded tax incentives for dairy processing facilities in Pennsylvania, including lowering investment and job-creation thresholds to qualify for an existing tax credit program. Both amendments were defeated 102-101 along party lines. A minor technical amendment by Stender regarding milk measurement passed unanimously.
